coredata.dc:title: Employing metformin-directed carbon dots with room-temperature phosphorescent towards the dual-channel detection of L-tryptophan
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Zhiying
Wang, Junchen
Chen, Xin
Liu, Jie
Zhu, Ying
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Currently, carbon dots (CDs) with the eminent phosphorescence have been extensively concerned owing to their more widespread applications. Herein, we employed metformin as the carbon source and successfully synthesized one kind of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) CDs doped with the elements of N, P through a facile microwave method. Significantly, the RTP emission of CDs appeared while the solution was fixed on the filter paper. To be specific, the hydrogen bonds formed between CDs and the filter paper, which led to the restriction of molecular rotations and motions. Again, the non-radiation attenuation rate of the excited triplet state was reduced by rigidifying the whole system, thus inhibiting the non-radiative transitions and boosting their RTP emission CDs. Interestingly, the proposed CDs could serve as the RTP ink and was applied to paint various patterns and prepare CDs-PVA film with both fluorescence and phosphorescence. Moreover, both their fluorescence and phosphorescence of these CDs was obviously enhanced by introducing L-tryptophan, thus establishing an innovative dual-channel detection of L-trp. Besides, the detection mechanism was also explored, and the increased hydroxyl-groups enhanced their fluorescence of CDs through the radiative recombination by L-trp, while the phosphorescence was enhanced by the narrowed the energy gap (∆E ST), thus promoting the singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing (ISC).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorescence
RTP ink
Dual-channel detection
L-tryptophan

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs loaded on the filter paper
Investigation of selectivity and potential interference
Synthesis and optical property of CDs
Fluorescence and phosphorescence stability of CDs
Luminescent response of CDs to L-tryptophan
Selectivity and potential interference for CDs assaying L-tryptophan
Establishing the dual-channel assay of L-tryptophan
Mechanism of fluorescent and phosphorescent response of CDs for L-tryptophan

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Pharmacy Education of Southwest University , China
National Demonstration Center for Experimental Pharmacy Education of Southwest University, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing , China ( cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027 ), National Demonstration Center for Experimental Pharmacy Education of Southwest University , China ( YX2019-CXYB13 ).

We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027), National Demonstration Center for Experimental Pharmacy Education of Southwest University, China (YX2019-CXYB13).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of preparing CDs with both fluorescence and phosphorescence.

Phosphorescence of CDs with varied mass ratios of phosphoric acid to metformin (A) and reaction time (B); (C) FL spectrum of CDs (inset: images with (a) daylight and (b) UV) and RTP spectrum of CDs loaded on the paper (images with (c) UV on and (d) UV off); (D) FL 3D spectrum of CDs; (E) FL spectra of CDs with different excitations; (F) RTP spectra with different excitations; (G) UV–vis spectrum of CDs; (H) FL and (I) RTP lifetime decay of CDs.

(A) HR-TEM images of CDs; (B) Size distribution of CDs; (C) FTIR spectrum and (D) XPS survey of CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (E), N 1s (F), O 1s (G) and P 2p (H) for CDs; (I) XRD pattern of CDs.
(A) Images of CDs powder with daylight, 365 nm of UV on and UV off; (B) FL and Phos of PVA film with CDs; FL and Phos images of traffic sign (C) and Pigsy (D) painted by CDs, respectively.

(A) FL spectra of CDs without (b) or with (c) L-trp (inset: images of CDs under daylight (a) or UV (b), and CDs with L-trp under UV (c)); (D) Phos spectra of CDs without (a) or with (b) L-trp on the paper (inset: images of CD without (a) or with L-trp (b) while UV off); (B) Fluorescent and (E) phosphorescent ratios of CDs with different substance to CDs only; (C) Fluorescence and phosphorescence (F) variations of CDs with L-trp by adding various substance.

(A) FL spectra of CDs with varying concentrations of L-trp; (B) Plot of F/F
0 -1 versus various concentrations of L-trp; (C) Phos spectra of CDs with different concentrations of L-trp; (D) Plot of P/P
0 -1 versus diverse concentrations of L-trp.
(A) Zeta potentials of CD, L-trp and CDs with L-trp; (B) FTIR spectra of CDs without and with L-trp; (C) Schematic mechanism for the enhanced phosphorescence of CDs by L-trp.

head.ce:title: Employing metformin-directed carbon dots with room-temperature phosphorescent towards the dual-channel detection of L-tryptophan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, carbon dots (CDs) with the eminent phosphorescence have been extensively concerned owing to their more widespread applications. Herein, we employed metformin as the carbon source and successfully synthesized one kind of room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) CDs doped with the elements of N, P through a facile microwave method. Significantly, the RTP emission of CDs appeared while the solution was fixed on the filter paper. To be specific, the hydrogen bonds formed between CDs and the filter paper, which led to the restriction of molecular rotations and motions. Again, the non-radiation attenuation rate of the excited triplet state was reduced by rigidifying the whole system, thus inhibiting the non-radiative transitions and boosting their RTP emission CDs. Interestingly, the proposed CDs could serve as the RTP ink and was applied to paint various patterns and prepare CDs-PVA film with both fluorescence and phosphorescence. Moreover, both their fluorescence and phosphorescence of these CDs was obviously enhanced by introducing L-tryptophan, thus establishing an innovative dual-channel detection of L-trp. Besides, the detection mechanism was also explored, and the increased hydroxyl-groups enhanced their fluorescence of CDs through the radiative recombination by L-trp, while the phosphorescence was enhanced by the narrowed the energy gap (∆E ST), thus promoting the singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing (ISC).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data is available upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence exhibits the superiority on the basis of longer lifetime and wider stokes shift. However, it is challengeable to acquire RTP materials due to their spin-forbidden of triplet exciton transitions [1,2]. So far, organometallic complexes and organic compounds without metal are considered as the conventional RTP materials, both of which generally require the complicated synthesis procedures or the high-cost, and usually show the obvious toxicity [3,4]. Therefore, it is greatly worthwhile to develop new phosphorescent materials.

Carbon Dots, showing kinds of advantages such as the stable luminescence, facile synthesis, favorable biocompatibility, have been recognized as a promising class of fluorescent nanomaterials [5]. In recent years, the current room temperature phosphorescence of CDs indeed activated the widespread interest, and these CDs display the attractive prospect in the high-tech fields of biological imaging, optical recording, and anti-counterfeiting technique [6–9]. Accordingly, the phosphorescent CDs obtain the broader opportunities due to their involvement of singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing (ISC), which leads to their decay rates comparatively slower [7,10]. Basically, there emerged multiple ways of synthesizing phosphorescent CDs. First, CDs-based RTP materials are synthesized through cooperating CDs with the certain polymer materials (such as poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) [11], silica gel [12], polyurethane (PU) [13] or polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) [14]), where CDs form the hydrogen bonds with the matrix, [15], thus fixing the structure and stimulating the stability of the excited triplets. Another method is achieved through introducing nitrogen-containing heterocycles, aromatic carbonyl groups or heavy halogens, which incredibly enhances ISC from S1 to Tn by the effective spin orbit coupling (SOC) or little single-triplet energy gap (ΔE ST) [16–19]. Recently, a new way of directly synthesizing RTP-CDs by one step appeared. Generally, the raw materials are utilized to prepared CDs and simultaneously doped with the elements (such as N, P, S), which are mainly introduced for enhancing the process of SOC. In addition, doping the elements of N, P, or halogen into CDs promote the occurrence of n-π*transition, thus facilitating the excitons to be triplet species through ISC process [16,20,21]. Hence, this strategy is considered as a maneuverable way of preparing RTP-CDs.

L-tryptophan (L-trp), as one of the essential amino acids, is the precursor of neurotransmitter serotonin (5-HT), which affects the diversified aspects of the brain activity such as the regulation of emotion, energy and memory. Besides, L-trp plays a vital role in various biological processes, and the varying amounts of L-trp are directly associated with multiple diseases and physiological metabolites [22]. Currently, UV–vis, high performance liquid chromatography [23], capillary electrophoresis [24] and fluorescent spectroscopy [25] are applied to monitor the amount of L-trp, which mainly require either the complicated operations or the interference elimination from other amino acids. Therefore, developing the facile method of detecting L-trp remains valuable.

In this study, we proposed an innovative kind of CDs doped with the element of N and P, which showed the fluorescence (FL) and RTP emissions by using the microwave synthesis and metformin and phosphoric acid as the raw materials, and their green RTP of these CDs lasted up to 7 s while existing as the solid. Importantly, the hydrogen bonds formed when CDs were fixed on the filter paper, which inhibited their molecular motions and promoted the process of ISC, thus the green RTP signal emerging ( Fig. 1). Moreover, the introduction of L-trp into CDs resulted in the enhanced fluorescence and phosphorescence, and a dual-signal assay of L-trp was accordingly established to with the satisfactory selectivity. Besides, we employed the CDs prepared here as RTP ink to draw the various patterns, which provided their possibility for the application in information encryption.

Metformin hydrochloride was purchased from Beijing Jinming biology science and technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), and phosphoric acid (H3PO4), poly (vinyl alcohol) [PVA], L-tryptophan, cholesterol, glutathione reduced (GSH), N, N-Dimethylformamide, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), L-lysine, L-valine, were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Also, trypsin was acquired from Beijing Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Similarly, Lactose was bought from Beijing InnoChem Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) as well as Vitamin C from Shanghai yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Again, melamine, L-arginine, L-cysteine were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Further, L-histidine, L-alanine, L-glutamic acid, glucose, Vitamin B2, methanol, ethanol, n-propanol, n-butanol, acetone, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, barium chloride (BaCl2) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). All these commercial reagents were analytical grade and directly utilized with no further purification.

All the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra were measured at 690 V of PMT Voltage (Ex slit: 2.5 nm, Em slit: 5 nm), while the phosphorescence spectra were obtained at 690 V of PMT Voltage and 40 Hz of Chopping speed (Ex slit: 10 nm, Em slit: 20 nm). Then, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) data were conducted by a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200 kV, and the size distribution was performed by using a Zeta-sizer Nano Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern, England). Likewise, the UV-Vis absorption spectra were performed by the Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Next, a ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (New York, America) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Tokyo, Japan) were employed to identify the element composition and functional groups of CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was provided with a Rigaku Ministar instrument (Tokyo, Japan). Again, the microwave oven of Galanz was utilized in the experiments (Shunde, China), and the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetime of P-CDs at excitation of 370 nm were obtained by an Edinburgh FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Paris, France). Meanwhile, the quantum yield was identified by using a quantum efficiency measurement system C9920–02 G (Hamamatsu, Japan). Further, the pH values of the solutions were measured by a Fangzhong pHS-3 C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China), and all the separations were achieved by the centrifuge (TG16-W) of Cence instrument Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China).

Initially, 1.2 g of metformin was dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water, and 0.5 mL of phosphoric acid was added to the previous solution, and the mixture was stirred for 2 min. Next, the formed transparent solution was transferred to an evaporating dish and heated by a microwave oven at 700 W for 2 min. Upon cooling to room temperature, the crude product appeared as the pale-yellow solid followed by dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and 2 M of NaOH was introduced to adjust the pH to neutral. Then, the large or agglomerated particles were removed by the centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was filtered via 0.22 µm of millipore membrane to acquire the purified CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs loaded on the filter paper
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.5 mg of CDs solid was added to 5 mL of ultrapure water, and its pH was adjusted to neutral by NaOH, and the supernatant was collected through the centrifugation and filtration. Then, 100 μL of the precious supernatant was pipetted and dropped on the filter paper, and the paper was completely dried after being placed in vacuum dryer at 80 °C for 2 h.

Routinely, 0.1 mg mL−1 of CDs (each for 100 μL) were pipetted into the parallel Eppendorf (EP) tubes of 1.5 mL, and each tube was separately added 100 μL of L-trp with various concentrations. Then, these solutions were diluted to 1000 μL by ultrapure water with thoroughly vortex, and each solution was incubated to completely react under the optimized temperature and time (20 °C and 40 min). Next, the fluorescence of these solutions was measured, respectively. Meanwhile, 100 μL of each solution was dropped on the filter paper, and their phosphorescence was recored after drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of selectivity and potential interference
ce:section.ce:para: During investigating the selectivity of CDs detecting L-trp, 200 μL of CDs and 600 μL of water were parallelly pipetted into 1.5 mL of EP tubes. Subsequently, 200 μL of various related substance (L-tryptophan, L-alanine, L-lysine, L-valine, L-argnine, L-tyrosine, L-glycine, L-glutamic acid, lactose, nicotinic acid, each at 10−2 M) were respectively introduced into the previously prepared solutions followed by thorough mixing. After the incubation of these solutions at 40 °C for 20 min, their fluorescence was measured. Meanwhile, 100 μL of these solutions were dropped on the filter paper and subjected to the phosphorescent measurement. Similarly, the interference from possibly coexisting substance and ions on the detection of L-trp (10−2 M) was evaluated upon the testing conditions. In particular, 200 μL of CDs, 600 μL of ultrapure water and 200 μL of various substance (melamine, cholesterol, glucose, Vitamin B2, GSH, trypsin, ATP, Na+, K+, each for 10−2 M) were introduced to 1.5 mL of EP tubes, respectively. Accordingly, their fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of these mixtures were performed by following the previous steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical property of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Basically, the synthetic conditions of CDs were optimized to obtain the optimal phosphorescence (Phos) by studying different mass ratio of phosphoric acid to metformin and reaction time. As shown in Fig. 2A, CDs were prepared with the adjustable mass ratios of phosphoric acid to metformin, demonstrating that their mass ratio of 1:1.5 showed the highest intensity of RTP. Similarly, CDs emitted the maximal Phos with the reaction time of 2 min (Fig. 2B). Thus, the mass ratio of 1:1.5 and 2 min were selected. Accordingly, the solution of CDs only exhibited the blue FL with the maximum FL excitation and emission of 328 nm and 376 nm, and their phosphorescence with the maximum RTP excitation and emission of 325 nm and 496 nm was observed after being coated on the filter paper (Fig. 2C). Meanwhile, the optimal FL excitation and emission of CDs solution were confirmed through the 3D spectral scanning (Fig. 2D). Again, both their fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs were obviously excitation-dependent (Fig. 2E and F). To be specific, their FL emission showed a slight red-shift stimulated by the excitations varying from 318 nm to 378 nm, and only the intensity of RTP was affected by various excitations from 285 nm to 355 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 2G, the prepared CDs showed an absorption band at 262 nm, which was resulted from π-π * transitions of CN groups [26]. Furthermore, the FL and Phos decay spectra of CDs were obtained (Fig. 2H and I), and their lifetime were calculated as 6.03 ns and 613.03 ms (Table S1 and S2).

To directly observe the morphology and particle size of CDs, the HR-TEM image was initially acquired, and the CDs were well-dispersed and spherical in aqueous ( Fig. 3A), which was subsequently confirmed by the DLS analysis with the size distribution of 5.45–8.44 nm (Fig. 3B). Simultaneously, there existed the crystal surface spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 3A illustration), which was originated from the (100) facet of graphite carbon [27,28].

To clarify the phosphorescent behavior of CDs, FTIR and XPS were introduced to explore their functional groups and chemical compositions. Primarily, there displayed a broad FTIR absorption band at approximately 2500–3500 cm−1 for CDs (Fig. 3C), which consisted of the distinguishable peaks at 3142 and 2841 cm−1 stemmed from the stretching vibrations of N-H and C-H groups [7]. The peak of 1670 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CN or bending vibration of N-H [3], while the peaks located at 1400, 1260, 1080, and 1009 cm−1 were ascribed to the bending vibration of C-H, stretching vibrations of C-N, P-O-C, and P-N, respectively [29]. Again, the peak of 509 nm was resulted from the stretching vibration of PO4 - [30,31]. To determine the element composition of CDs, their XPS survey analysis was thereupon performed. These CDs were mainly composed of the elements including C, N, O, and P (Fig. 3D), and the content of which were 37.07%, 18.28%, 31.84% and 12.81%, respectively. More precisely, the high resolution XPS survey exhibited three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.6 eV for C 1s (Fig. 3E), indicating the existence of C-N/C-P, C-O and O-CO for the current CDs. Meanwhile, three peaks of N 1s spectrum appeared at 399.3 eV, 401.0 eV and 402.1 eV (Fig. 3F), which were associated with C-NC, C-N/N-P and N-H, agreeing with that of the FTIR data. Also, two peaks of O 1s spectrum were separately assigned to C-O-C (530.7 eV) and P-O (532.5 eV) (Fig. 3G), and two peaks at 133.9 eV and 134.9 eV for P 2p were originated from P-O and P-N/P-C (Fig. 3H) [3,7,30,32]. In addition, there emerged a peak in the 2θ range of 26.5° for the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 3I), revealing the highly disordered carbon atoms like the graphite lattice spacing [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and phosphorescence stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, we explored the optical stability of CDs towards various conditions. Briefly, the FL intensity of CDs hardly exhibited the variation with different exposure time of UV (Fig. S1A), temperature (Fig. S1C), concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S1D) and metal ions (Fig. S1E). Howbeit, the FL intensity of CDs slightly increased with pH rising (Fig. S1B), and the organic solvents introduced here barely affected their FL intensity except acetone and DMF (Fig. S1F). To evaluate their Phos stability of CDs, the potential impact of pH, metal ions and organic solvents were studied. As shown in Fig. S2A, the Phos intensity of CDs was slightly affected by pH ranging from 2 to 11, and the metal ions and organic solvents exhibited the generally acceptable influence on their Phos (Fig. S2B and S2C). Taken together, these findings demonstrated that CDs showed the satisfactory fluorescence and phosphorescence stability.

Basically, the powder of CDs with the natural light emerged as light-yellow, and emitted an obvious blue fluorescence under 365 nm of UV light ( Fig. 4A). Upon the UV switching off, the attractive long room temperature phosphorescence was visible up to 7 s by naked eyes (Fig. 4A). Furthermore, the CDs prepared here were loaded in the solution of PVA with stirring followed by drying in the oven, and the dried PVA incorporated with CDs showed the blue fluorescence under UV, while its green phosphorescence still appeared with UV off (Fig. 4B). This phenomenon provided a new way of producing PVA films with phosphorescence, showing the potential of serving as one new kind of anti-counterfeiting material since the aqueous dispersion of PVA with CDs was colorless. Inspired by two-channel signals of CDs, we applied the proposed CDs as the RTP ink for graphic paintings. Two intricate patterns of the traffic sign (Fig. 4C) and the major character of Pigsy in Western Travels (Fig. 4D) were painted with CDs. Accordingly, all these patterns were clearly observed with the bright blue FL under UV of 365 nm and the green RTP signals with UV off. Overall, the previous evidence proved that the CDs described here could be employed as a new type of luminous anti-counterfeiting material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent response of CDs to L-tryptophan
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the abundant functional groups of CDs and indole structure of L-trp, we therefore studied the interaction between CDs and L-trp. Hence, L-trp was introduced into the aqueous solution of CDs, and both their fluorescence and phosphorescence signals of CDs were obviously enhanced by about 63% ( Fig. 5A and 5D). This phenomenon suggested that CDs showed the possibility of establishing the dual-channel detection strategy of L-trp. Subsequently, we optimized the fluorescent detecting conditions for L-trp, and a series experiments were performed. As illustrated in Fig. S3, 40 min, 20 ℃ and pH = 9 was selected as the optimal conditions of CDs assaying L-trp towards both the further fluorescent and phosphorescent detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and potential interference for CDs assaying L-tryptophan
ce:section.ce:para: To further identify the selectivity of CDs detecting L-trp, a series of related substance (L-alanine, L-lysine, L-valine, L-argnine, L-tyrosine, L-glycine, L-glutamic acid, lactose and nicotinic acid, each for 0.1 mM) were investigated. The results indicated that the fluorescence of CDs was obviously enhanced by L-trp rather than other substance (Fig. 5B), while their phosphorescence rose of CDs by only L-trp (Fig. 5E), demonstrating that CDs exhibited a generally satisfactory selectivity towards assaying L-trp. Likewise, we tested the potential effect of different substance on CDs assaying L-trp. Diverse substance (melamine, cholesterol, glucose, vitamin B2, GSH, trypsin, ATP, Na+ and K+, each for 0.1 mM) were added into CDs with L-trp introduced. As shown in Fig. 5C and F, both the FL and Phos intensity of CDs with L-trp were barely affected by these substance, suggesting that this dual-channel assay of L-trp showed the satisfactory anti-interference performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing the dual-channel assay of L-tryptophan
ce:section.ce:para: To completely build up the assay of L-trp on the basis of CDs, a series of concentrations of L-trp were employed. Importantly, the FL intensity of CDs was regularly increased with the amounts of L-trp ranging from 0.4 μM to 700 μM (R2 = 0.997), and the corresponding detection limit was determined to be 41.2 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 ( Fig. 6A and 6B). Likewise, their Phos intensity of CDs was gradually strengthened with the increasing amounts of L-trp from 2 μM to 700 μM (R2 = 0.988) with a detection limit of 66.7 nM (Fig. 6C and D). In short, we successfully established a new detecting approach for L-trp based on their FL and Phos response of CDs to L-trp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescent and phosphorescent response of CDs for L-tryptophan
ce:section.ce:para: For elucidating the mechanism of CDs detecting L-tryptophan, the further explorations were accomplished. To verify the interaction between CDs and L-trp, we determined the zeta potentials, and the zeta potential of CDs decreased from − 2.9 mV to − 6.8 mV after the introduction of L-trp ( Fig. 7A). This finding proved that CDs showed the interaction with L-trp. To further clarify the fluorescence enhancement of CDs by L-trp, we compared the FTIR of CDs and that of CDs with L-trp. As shown in Fig. 7B, the new absorption band at 3415 cm−1 appeared after adding L-trp, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH. Increased hydroxyl-group on the surface of CDs could enhance FL, which was originated from radiative recombination. More specifically, when more hydroxyl groups appeared on the surface of CDs, the radiative recombination between electrons and holes occurred by the illumination of excitation light, thereby enhancing the FL [35,36].

To further explore the possible phosphorescence enhancement mechanism of CDs with L-tryptophan, the energy levels of S1 and T1 states for CDs and CDs with L-trp were calculated by their FL and Phos spectra (Fig. 5A and D). For CDs, the energy levels of S1 = 1240/362 eV and T1 = 1240/496 eV were calculated to be 3.425 and 2.500 eV, and the singlet-triplet energy gap (∆E ST) were acquired as 0.925 eV (∆E ST = E S− E T). Through the electron transfer occurring between CDs and L-tryptophan, the energy levels of S1’ = 1240/353 eV and T1’ = 1240/446 eV were 3.513 and 2.780 eV, and the value of ∆E ST’ was 0.733 eV, which was lower than that of CDs only (Fig. 7C). Though calculating the triple energy gap which was necessary for the phosphorescence emission of CDs and CDs with L-trp, the ISC process was promoted after adding L-trp, thus leading to the phosphorescence enhancement.

In summary, a facile and low-cost strategy for the preparation of CDs with room temperature phosphorescence has been developed. The synthetic CDs appeared as the light-yellow solid and showed the blue fluorescence with UV of 365 nm, and they also emitted the green phosphorescence up to 7s by naked eyes once the UV lamp was switched off. Encouraged by their favorable FL and RTP feature, we employed the current CDs to paint kinds of patterns as one type of promising phosphorescent ink. Owing to their multiple functional groups, the CDs prepared here showed the obvious interaction with L-trp. After introducing the L-trp, the increased hydroxyl-groups on the surface of CDs promoted their FL by the radiative recombination, and the ISC was facilitated through the reduction of energy gap (∆E ST), thus enhancing their phosphorescence. By virtue of their enhanced fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs with introducing L-trp, we successfully established a dual-channel detecting strategy of L-trp with the satisfactory linearity, selectivity and anti-interference, thus providing a new way of assaying L-trp by the dual-channel signals.

Zhiying Feng: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Junchen Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xin Chen: Data curation, Software, Validation. Jie Liu: Data curation, Software, Validation. Ying Zhu: Supervision. Xiaoming Yang: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027), National Demonstration Center for Experimental Pharmacy Education of Southwest University, China (YX2019-CXYB13).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2021.112236.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.H. Chen, M.T. Zheng, Y. Xiao, H.W. Dong, H.R. Zhang, J.L. Zhuang, H. Hu, B.F. Lei, Y.L. Liu, A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 312–318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategy for activating room-temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in aqueous environments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Gao, H.L. Zhang, Y. Jiao, W.J. Lu, Y. Liu, H. Han, X.J. Gong, S.M. Shuang, C. Dong, Strategy for activating room-temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in aqueous environments, Chem. Mater. 31 (2019) 7979–7986.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of metal-free polymer carbon dots: a new class of room-temperature phosphorescent materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Tao, S.Y. Lu, Y.J. Geng, S.J. Zhu, S.A. T. Redfern, Y.B. Song, T.L. Feng, W.Q. Xu, B. Yang, Design of metal-free polymer carbon dots: a new class of room-temperature phosphorescent materials, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 57 (2018) 2393–2398.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-long room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots: pH sensing and dual-channel detection of tetracyclines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.S. Lu, Q. Su, X.M. Yang, Ultra-long room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots: pH sensing and dual-channel detection of tetracyclines, Nanoscale 11 (2019) 16036–16042.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid-state carbon dots with red fluorescence and efficient construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies
